A system is built on the assumption that it can generate candidate ideas and criticize them mostly in language. That works only if the target novelty is itself a verbal rearrangement of existing concepts. But one stated edge case is precisely when novelty depends on inventing a new measurement or a new experimental protocol. In that case, verbal search is not enough, because the thing to be discovered is not merely a better description; it is a new way of making the world answer.

A second limit appears when the needed advance is mathematical rather than discursive. Einstein’s route from an initial insight to field equations took ten years of mathematics. If that path cannot be approximated by verbal search, then an architecture centered on linguistic candidate-generation will stall at the level of suggestive slogans. It may produce “happiest thoughts” without the machinery needed to turn them into consequences, constraints, or equations. So in at least two classes of domains—protocol invention and deep mathematical derivation—the architecture is structurally mismatched to the work.

The pool structure creates another failure mode. If the background pool, universal pool, and novel pool are mixed incorrectly, the system does not escape the basin of known ideas. Instead it launders familiar fragments into outputs that look new because their pieces are recombined under fresh wording. Since generation and criticism are mostly verbal, that laundering may pass internal checks: the explanation sounds different enough to count as a candidate, while never departing from inherited materials.

The remaining premises illustrate what happens when evidence is obvious and when a trace is unstable but still followed. Almost everyone reacted to the mince because its soaked presence was obvious. In such a world, some signals are so salient that no sophisticated search is needed; nearly everyone converges immediately. The architecture’s elaborate language loop adds little there. By contrast, the pilgrims followed wobbly catfish footprints to the boot. The trail is real enough to be followed, but it is irregular and indirect. That is a picture of inquiry under weak guidance: agents proceed from ambiguous marks toward an object whose connection to the marks may be hard to justify in language alone.

Put together, the world implied by these premises contains three regimes. In one, evidence is obvious, and almost everyone reacts. In another, evidence is wobbly, and agents can still track it toward a target. In the hardest regime, success requires new measurements, new protocols, or sustained mathematics. There the linguistic architecture is least reliable. If it also mixes its pools badly, then instead of reaching the boot by a genuinely new path, it circles inside known terrain and reports that the circle is discovery.
